contents.result: boo/README.md

# Boo

Boo is a headless CLI for driving interactive terminal programs through a real
PTY. The only supported control surface is the `boo` command; the daemon keeps
the native session machinery private behind that CLI boundary.

## Build

```bash
cmake -S . -B build
cmake --build build -j2
```

The native build depends on `ghostty-vt` and no longer depends on SDL3 or
font/rendering libraries.

## CLI

```bash
uv run boo start "bash"
uv run boo screen
uv run boo type $'echo hello\n'
uv run boo press enter
uv run boo alive
uv run boo stop
```

`boo start` launches a background daemon that owns a single headless session.
Every other CLI command connects to that daemon over a Unix domain socket,
issues one command, prints the result, and exits.

## Tests

```bash
make test
```

`ctest` runs the native smoke test. A higher-level CLI smoke script also lives
in `tests/test_cli.py`, but it requires permissions to bind a local Unix socket
in the current environment.

docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-15-## How we got here
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-16-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:17:The original plan put model validation and persistence in `OxApp.SubmitInput()` with a dedicated `ModelCompleter` class. Architectural review identified three problems:
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-18-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:19:1. **Command execution leaked into the TUI layer.** `/model` is a configuration concern, not a TUI concern. Validation ("is this model ID known?") and persistence (`SetSelectedModelAsync`) are business logic that belongs in Ur, not Ox. OxApp should dispatch and display â€” not decide what constitutes a valid model.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-20-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:21:2. **Dual command interception got worse.** OxApp already intercepts built-in commands at line 314 (stubs), and `UrSession.TryExpandSlashCommand` intercepts them again at line 314 (log and swallow). The original plan doubled down on OxApp as the execution point, making the session-layer interception even more vestigial.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-22-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-23-3. **`ModelCompleter` hardcoded command knowledge.** A UI completion class that checks for the literal string `/model ` couples it to a specific command name. When `/set` needs argument completion, you'd need another parallel class.
--
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-25-## Approaches considered
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-26-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:27:### Option A â€” ModelCompleter in Ox, execution in OxApp
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-28-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:29:- Summary: Dedicated `ModelCompleter` class. Model validation and `SetSelectedModelAsync` called directly from OxApp.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-30-- Pros: Simple. Few files changed.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:31:- Cons: Business logic in TUI layer. Hardcoded command name in completer. Deepens the dual-dispatch problem.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:32:- Failure modes: Every new built-in command that needs arguments requires a new completer class and more business logic in OxApp.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-33-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-34-### Option B â€” General argument completions in AutocompleteEngine, execution in UrSession
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-35-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:36:- Summary: Extend `AutocompleteEngine` to accept per-command argument completion lists. Add `ExecuteBuiltInCommand` to `UrSession` for command execution. OxApp dispatches and displays.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-37-- Pros: Autocomplete is general-purpose (no command-specific classes). Business logic stays in Ur. Clean layer boundary. `/set` gets argument completion for free later by adding a dictionary entry.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-38-- Cons: Slightly more work upfront. `AutocompleteEngine` gains a second responsibility (argument completion alongside command-name completion).
--
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-41-## Recommended approach
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-42-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:43:**Option B.** The extra upfront work is small (one dictionary parameter, one method on UrSession, one result type) and it respects the existing layer boundaries. The autocomplete engine stays command-agnostic â€” it just knows that some commands have completable argument lists. Command execution stays in Ur where it has access to configuration and validation logic. OxApp stays thin: dispatch and display.

docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md:73:**Hierarchy.** Command execution moves from Ox (TUI) to Ur (core). OxApp calls down into `UrSession.ExecuteBuiltInCommand()` and displays the result â€” same direction as `StartTurn` calling `RunTurnAsync`. No upward dependency introduced.

The two phases are distinguished by the presence of a space in the input â€” a clean structural boundary, not a special case.

docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-007-model-command.md-77-**Modularization.** `ExecuteBuiltInCommand` on `UrSession` is the natural home â€” the session already holds `_configuration` and `_builtInCommands`. docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-004-zai-coding-provider.md:26:Four production providers exist: OpenRouter, OpenAI, Google, Ollama. The OpenAI provider is the simplest â€” it uses the OpenAI .NET SDK (`OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient`) with a static context window table and keyring-backed API key. Z.AI's API is OpenAI-protocol-compatible, so the same SDK works with a custom endpoint.
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-004-zai-coding-provider.md-27-
docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-004-zai-coding-provider.md:28:The `OpenAI.Chat.ChatClient` constructor accepts an `OpenAIClientOptions` parameter with an `Endpoint` property for overriding the base URL. The OpenAI provider currently uses the two-argument constructor (model + credential), so the Z.AI provider will use the three-argument form (model + credential + options) to pass `https://api.z.ai/api/coding/paas/v4` as the endpoint.

docs/agents/plans/2026-04-11-004-zai-coding-provider.md:34:**Abstraction**: The feature is at the right level. The Z.AI provider is a concrete provider implementation; the rest of the system interacts through `IProvider`. There's no temptation to create an "OpenAI-compatible base class" â€” the shared code is tiny (a few lines of SDK construction), and premature abstraction would add complexity for two call sites.

Now I have a comprehensive understanding. Based on my exploration, this is **Ox** - a TUI (terminal user interface) AI agent system built in .NET.

## Key components:

1. **Ox** (`src/Ox/`) - The main TUI application with terminal UI components, conversation views, input handling, and rendering
2. **Ur** (`src/Ur/`) - The core agent engine providing:
   - Agent loop and session management
   - Tool system (bash, file operations, glob, grep, etc.)
   - Provider abstraction for different LLM backends (OpenAI, Google, Ollama, etc.)
   - Permission system and skill framework
3. **Te** (`src/Te/`) - Terminal emulation library for input handling and rendering

## Main features:

- **TUI interface** with conversation display, input area, and status bar
- **Multi-provider support** (OpenAI, Google Gemini, Ollama, OpenRouter, Z.AI)
- **Headless mode** for automated tasks and evaluations
- **Tool system** for file operations, bash commands, grep, etc.
- **Skill framework** for extensible commands
- **Permission system** for tool access control
- **Session persistence** and context management
- **Evaluation framework** for testing agent behavior

## Architecture:

- **Ox** (presentation layer): TUI rendering, input handling, conversation display
- **Ur** (business logic): Agent loop, tool orchestration, provider management
- **Providers** (integration): Adapters for different LLM APIs
- **Boo** (testing): Headless terminal driver for automated testing

The project appears to be a sophisticated CLI/TUI-based AI coding assistant that runs locally, integrates with multiple LLM providers, and can perform complex software engineering tasks through a tool-equipped agent system.

contents.result:
# Boo

Boo is a headless CLI for driving interactive terminal programs through a real
PTY. The only supported control surface is the `boo` command; the daemon keeps
the native session machinery private behind that CLI boundary.

## Build

```bash
cmake -S . -B build
cmake --build build -j2
```

The native build depends on `ghostty-vt` and no longer depends on SDL3 or
font/rendering libraries.

## CLI

```bash
uv run boo start "bash"
uv run boo screen
uv run boo type $'echo hello\n'
uv run boo press enter
uv run boo alive
uv run boo stop
```

`boo start` launches a background daemon that owns a single headless session.
Every other CLI command connects to that daemon over a Unix domain socket,
issues one command, prints the result, and exits.

## Tests

```bash
make test
```

`ctest` runs the native smoke test. A higher-level CLI smoke script also lives
in `tests/test_cli.py`, but it requires permissions to bind a local Unix socket
in the current environment.
